unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Relating pre-thermodynamics engineering to later theory
question_text: Early steam engines were developed and improved before scientists fully formulated the laws of thermodynamics, which shows that?
answer: practical technology can advance through trial and improvement before complete theoretical models exist

choices.text: practical technology can advance through trial and improvement before complete theoretical models exist
thermodynamics laws are irrelevant to any real engine design
steam engines violate conservation of energy
no energy conversions occur in historical machines

explanation: Unit B emphasizes that engineering practice often precedes formal theory.
common_mistake: Students think laws must be known before any useful device can exist.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Describing Watt steam engine efficiency improvement
question_text: James Watt's improvements to the steam engine increased efficiency partly by _______?
answer: adding a separate condenser so the main cylinder stayed hotter

choices.text: adding a separate condenser so the main cylinder stayed hotter
eliminating all use of steam pressure
converting the engine to run on nuclear fission
removing all moving parts from the design

explanation: Separate condensing reduced heat loss in the cylinder, improving performance.
common_mistake: Students think Watt invented steam power from scratch rather than improving efficiency.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Identifying primary energy conversion in automobiles
question_text: The development of the automobile depended on efficient conversion of _______?
answer: chemical energy in fuel to mechanical energy for wheels

choices.text: chemical energy in fuel to mechanical energy for wheels
gravitational energy in tires to nuclear energy in axles
only static electric charge in the frame
thermal energy in air to permanent magnetism in glass

explanation: Internal combustion converts fuel chemical energy to mechanical motion.
common_mistake: Students overlook the fuel-to-motion conversion chain in vehicles.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Describing energy conversion in historical windmill
question_text: Windmills used historically for grinding grain converted?
answer: kinetic energy of moving air to mechanical rotation of millstones

choices.text: kinetic energy of moving air to mechanical rotation of millstones
nuclear energy in blades to chemical energy in flour only
gravitational energy of clouds to electrical energy directly
thermal energy of stones to wind with no moving parts

explanation: Wind turns blades; mechanical linkage drives grinding stones.
common_mistake: Students confuse historical windmills with modern electrical wind turbines only.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Linking conversion efficiency to environmental impact
question_text: From an environmental STS perspective, inefficient energy conversions matter because they _______?

choices.text: increase fuel consumption and greenhouse gas emissions for the same useful output
eliminate all waste heat from ecosystems permanently
have no connection to natural resource use
guarantee 100% useful work in all devices

explanation: Lower efficiency means more fuel burned and more environmental impact per task.
common_mistake: Students think efficiency is purely a physics lab concept unrelated to environment.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Applying economic perspective to energy efficiency
question_text: When analyzing a technology from an economic STS perspective, efficiency affects?
answer: operating costs because less efficient devices consume more energy per unit of useful output

choices.text: operating costs because less efficient devices consume more energy per unit of useful output
only the colour of the device casing
the number of chromosomes in the operator
the speed of light in vacuum exclusively

explanation: Higher efficiency reduces energy bills and long-term operating expense.
common_mistake: Students separate economic analysis from energy efficiency entirely.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems

choices.text: using natural resources responsibly and avoiding waste out of stewardship for the land
maximizing resource extraction without any limits always
rejecting all technology regardless of context
ignoring environmental consequences of energy choices

explanation: Alberta curriculum includes stewardship and judicious resource use in STS outcomes.
common_mistake: Students think STS perspectives exclude Indigenous stewardship viewpoints.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Evaluating lighting technologies using multiple STS criteria
question_text: Comparing incandescent, fluorescent, and LED lighting involves evaluating?
answer: efficiency, cost, lifespan, and environmental impact of each technology

choices.text: efficiency, cost, lifespan, and environmental impact of each technology
only the wattage printed on packaging with no other factors
the genetic sequence of the glass filament
whether bulbs float in water exclusively

explanation: STS analysis weighs multiple criteria when choosing lighting technology.
common_mistake: Students reduce comparison to a single numeric label.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Identifying pre-thermodynamics performance measures
question_text: Before thermodynamics was formalized, engineers measured machine performance using concepts such as _______?

choices.text: duty, horsepower, and fuel consumption per unit of work
quarks and leptons in piston assemblies
DNA base pairing in boiler tubes
magnetic monopoles in flywheels

explanation: Operational measures preceded abstract laws; performance was tracked practically.
common_mistake: Students think no quantitative performance measures existed before thermodynamics.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Comparing ecological and technological energy transfer efficiency
question_text: A food chain in an ecosystem differs from an electrical circuit as an energy system because in the food chain?
answer: energy is transferred between organisms and much is lost as heat at each trophic level

choices.text: energy is transferred between organisms and much is lost as heat at each trophic level
energy is conserved with 100% efficiency at every transfer
no energy transformations occur between levels
organisms create energy from nothing during photosynthesis and respiration

explanation: Biological energy transfers are inefficient compared to designed electrical systems.
common_mistake: Students apply 100% efficiency incorrectly to ecological energy transfer.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Applying energy conservation to find speed from height
question_text: A $60\ \text{kg}$ skier descends $10\ \text{m}$ vertically. Neglecting friction, the speed at the bottom is closest to (use $g = 9.8\ \text{m/s}^2$, energy conservation $mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$)?
answer: $14\ \text{m/s}$

choices.text: $14\ \text{m/s}$
$5\ \text{m/s}$
$98\ \text{m/s}$

explanation: $v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2(9.8)(10)} \approx 14\ \text{m/s}$; mass cancels.
common_mistake: Students include mass incorrectly or forget the square root step.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Defining power as rate of energy transfer
question_text: Power is defined as?
answer: the rate at which energy is transferred or transformed

choices.text: the rate at which energy is transferred or transformed
the total energy stored in an object at rest
mass times velocity only
displacement divided by force

explanation: Power = energy transfer / time; unit is the watt (J/s).
common_mistake: Students confuse power with total energy or force.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Calculating energy from power and time
question_text: A machine rated at $500\ \text{W}$ operates for $20\ \text{s}$. The energy transferred is _______?
explanation: Outcome B2.10k (Energy Flow in Technological Systems): Using values 500, 20, '$10\ 000\ \text{J}$' is correct because it matches calculating energy from power and time. Other options reflect common misconceptions in this unit.
common_mistake: Students divide power by time or add quantities incorrectly.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems

answer: removes mechanical energy from the box, often as thermal energy

choices.text: removes mechanical energy from the box, often as thermal energy
adds kinetic energy indefinitely without limit
has no effect on the box's energy
creates energy inside the box from nothing

explanation: Friction opposes motion and dissipates mechanical energy as heat.
common_mistake: Students think negative work means no energy change occurs.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Applying energy conservation between potential and kinetic forms
question_text: In a closed system with no external work, if potential energy decreases by $50\ \text{J}$, kinetic energy typically?
answer: increases by $50\ \text{J}$ if no friction converts energy to heat

choices.text: increases by $50\ \text{J}$ if no friction converts energy to heat
decreases by $50\ \text{J}$ always
remains unchanged in all circumstances
increases by $100\ \text{J}$ automatically

explanation: Conservation: $\Delta E_p + \Delta E_k = 0$ in ideal mechanical systems.
common_mistake: Students think both forms decrease together.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems

question_text: Heat engines such as car engines are limited in efficiency partly because _______?

choices.text: some energy must be expelled to a cooler reservoir as required by the second law
conservation of energy does not apply to engines
all fuel energy can become work with perfect pistons alone
cold reservoirs absorb zero energy in all cycles

explanation: Second law limits conversion of heat to work in cyclic engines.
common_mistake: Students blame only friction, ignoring fundamental thermodynamic limits.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
skill_tested: Analyzing transportation energy choices environmentally
question_text: Choosing bicycle transportation over a car for short trips can reduce environmental impact because _______?

choices.text: human-powered travel converts food chemical energy with lower fossil fuel consumption per trip
bicycles violate conservation of energy and create power from nothing
cars use no energy when idling at intersections
bicycles emit more CO₂ per kilometre than diesel trucks always

explanation: Active transport avoids direct fossil fuel burn for many short journeys.
common_mistake: Students think bicycles involve no energy considerations at all.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

choices.text: all cells arise from pre-existing cells
all organisms can arise from non-living matter
only plants are composed of cells
viruses are the smallest functional units of life

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

all life requires sunlight for energy
cells contain a nucleus in every organism

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Identifying Schleiden and Schwann contributions
question_text: Schleiden and Schwann contributed to cell theory by proposing that?
answer: plants and animals are composed of cells

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

answer: require a host cell to reproduce and lack independent metabolism

always contain chloroplasts for photosynthesis
are larger than most eukaryotic cells
have cell walls made of cellulose

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

answer: revealing ultrastructural details of organelles not visible with light microscopy

eliminating the need for staining techniques

proving that prokaryotes have nuclei

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

uses only visible light at magnifications below 10×
destroys all cellular membranes instantly
replaces the need for any knowledge of cell theory

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

how the Sun produces nuclear fusion in its core
how tectonic plates move continents
how atmospheric pressure creates weather fronts

explanation: Membrane transport research focuses on channels, pumps, and gradients at the cellular level.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

proves that cells can form from non-living chemicals in air

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

predict earthquakes from cell vibrations
measure the mass of entire ecosystems directly

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

perform photosynthesis using chlorophyll

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

synthesize lipids for the cell membrane only

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

choices.text: convert light energy into chemical energy during photosynthesis
break down fatty acids in the absence of oxygen

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

genetic stability by storing chromosomes
oxygen transport to mitochondria

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems

prevent any diffusion across membranes permanently
require spontaneous generation of new cells

explanation: Liposome bilayers mimic cell membranes, enabling targeted drug delivery via fusion or endocytosis.
common_mistake: Liposomes do not create chloroplasts or block all transport; they exploit membrane compatibility.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Explaining hormone entry as a membrane transport application
question_text: Some protein hormones enter target cells primarily by _______?
answer: diffusion through the membrane or receptor-mediated uptake when small enough or bound to carriers

choices.text: diffusion through the membrane or receptor-mediated uptake when small enough or bound to carriers
active photosynthesis in the hormone molecule itself
replacing the nucleus with a cell wall
osmosis moving hormones as water molecules only

explanation: Small, lipid-soluble hormones can diffuse across membranes; others bind surface receptors triggering internal signals.
common_mistake: Hormones are not water; they do not photosynthesize or replace nuclei.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating total microscope magnification
question_text: A compound light microscope has an eyepiece lens of 10× and an objective lens of 4×. What is the total magnification? Record a whole number.
explanation: Total magnification = eyepiece × objective = 10 × 4 = 40.
common_mistake: Students add lens powers instead of multiplying them.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating total microscope magnification
question_text: A compound light microscope has an eyepiece lens of 10× and an objective lens of 10×. What is the total magnification? Record a whole number.
explanation: Total magnification = eyepiece × objective = 10 × 10 = 100.
common_mistake: Students add lens powers instead of multiplying them.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating magnification from image and actual size
question_text: A specimen measures 30 μm across. Under the microscope the image appears 120 μm wide. What is the magnification? Record a whole number.
explanation: Magnification = image size ÷ actual size = 120/30 = 4.
common_mistake: Students divide actual by image size, inverting the ratio.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating magnification from image and actual size
question_text: A specimen measures 40 μm across. Under the microscope the image appears 200 μm wide. What is the magnification? Record a whole number.
explanation: Magnification = image size ÷ actual size = 200/40 = 5.
common_mistake: Students divide actual by image size, inverting the ratio.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating surface-area-to-volume ratio for a cubic cell
question_text: A cube-shaped cell has edges of 2 μm. Surface area = 6 × edge² and volume = edge³. What is the surface-area-to-volume ratio? Express as a decimal rounded to two decimal places.
common_mistake: Students invert the ratio (volume ÷ surface area) or forget to square/cube the edge.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating surface-area-to-volume ratio for a cubic cell
question_text: A cube-shaped cell has edges of 3 μm. Surface area = 6 × edge² and volume = edge³. What is the surface-area-to-volume ratio? Express as a decimal rounded to two decimal places.
common_mistake: Students invert the ratio (volume ÷ surface area) or forget to square/cube the edge.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating percent of plasmolyzed cells
question_text: In a microscope field of view, 12 of 50 plant cells show plasmolysis after placing the tissue in a hypertonic salt solution. What percent of cells are plasmolyzed? Express as a decimal rounded to one decimal place.
explanation: Outcome C1.4s (Cycling of Matter in Living Systems): Using values 12, 50, apply calculating percent of plasmolyzed cells to obtain 24.0.
common_mistake: Students divide total by plasmolyzed or forget to multiply by 100.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating percent of plasmolyzed cells
question_text: In a microscope field of view, 8 of 40 plant cells show plasmolysis after placing the tissue in a hypertonic salt solution. What percent of cells are plasmolyzed? Express as a decimal rounded to one decimal place.
explanation: Outcome C1.4s (Cycling of Matter in Living Systems): Using values 8, 40, apply calculating percent of plasmolyzed cells to obtain 20.0.
common_mistake: Students divide total by plasmolyzed or forget to multiply by 100.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating stomatal density from count and area
question_text: A leaf sample of 1 mm² contains 180 open stomata visible under the microscope. What is the stomatal density in stomata per mm²? Record a whole number.
explanation: Outcome C1.2s (Cycling of Matter in Living Systems): Using values 1, 180, apply calculating stomatal density from count and area to obtain 180.
common_mistake: Students multiply area by stomata count instead of dividing.

unit: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
topic: Cycling of Matter in Living Systems
skill_tested: Calculating stomatal density from count and area
question_text: A leaf sample of 2 mm² contains 240 open stomata visible under the microscope. What is the stomatal density in stomata per mm²? Record a whole number.
explanation: Outcome C1.2s (Cycling of Matter in Living Systems): Using values 2, 240, apply calculating stomatal density from count and area to obtain 120.
common_mistake: Students multiply area by stomata count instead of dividing.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Identifying the Sun as Earth's primary energy source
question_text: The primary source of energy driving Earth's weather, climate, and life processes is _______?
answer: electromagnetic radiation from the Sun

choices.text: electromagnetic radiation from the Sun
geothermal heat from Earth's core only
chemical energy stored in fossil fuels only
nuclear fusion occurring in the atmosphere

explanation: Solar radiation provides the continuous energy input that powers atmospheric circulation, the water cycle, and photosynthesis.
common_mistake: Geothermal and fossil fuels are secondary or stored energy; the Sun is the main external source.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Describing the form of incoming solar energy
question_text: Most of the Sun's energy reaching Earth arrives as?
answer: visible and near-infrared radiation

choices.text: visible and near-infrared radiation
gamma rays and X-rays only
sound waves through the vacuum of space
direct conduction through solid rock

explanation: The solar spectrum peaks in visible light, with significant near-infrared; high-energy radiation is largely filtered.
common_mistake: Space cannot transmit sound; most solar energy is EM radiation, not conduction.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Describing Earth's radiative energy balance
question_text: In Earth's energy budget, incoming solar radiation is balanced over time by _______?
answer: outgoing longwave (infrared) radiation to space

choices.text: outgoing longwave (infrared) radiation to space
complete storage of all energy in ocean sediments permanently
conversion of all sunlight into visible light reflected unchanged
elimination of all heat transfer from the surface

explanation: Earth absorbs shortwave solar energy and radiates longwave infrared back to space at approximately the same average rate.
common_mistake: Earth must radiate energy outward; it does not store all incoming solar energy indefinitely.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Explaining how clouds and ice affect solar energy distribution
question_text: Clouds and ice caps affect Earth's energy budget mainly by _______?
answer: reflecting a portion of incoming solar radiation back to space

choices.text: reflecting a portion of incoming solar radiation back to space
absorbing all ultraviolet radiation and converting it to mass
preventing any infrared radiation from leaving Earth
generating solar energy through nuclear fission

explanation: High-albedo surfaces such as clouds and snow reflect shortwave radiation, reducing energy absorbed at the surface.
common_mistake: Reflection reduces absorption; clouds do not create solar energy.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Defining albedo
question_text: Albedo is defined as the?
answer: fraction of incoming radiation reflected by a surface

choices.text: fraction of incoming radiation reflected by a surface
total amount of heat stored in an ocean current
rate of evaporation from a tropical rainforest
concentration of carbon dioxide in the troposphere

explanation: Albedo compares reflected radiation to incoming radiation for a surface or planet.
common_mistake: Albedo is a reflectivity ratio, not a heat storage or gas concentration measure.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Comparing albedo effects on surface energy absorption
question_text: Fresh snow has a higher albedo than a dark asphalt road, which means snow?
answer: reflects a greater percentage of incoming solar radiation

choices.text: reflects a greater percentage of incoming solar radiation
absorbs more solar energy and heats up faster always
emits no infrared radiation at any temperature
blocks all greenhouse gases from entering the atmosphere

explanation: Higher albedo means more reflection and less absorption of shortwave solar energy.
common_mistake: High albedo surfaces reflect more; they do not absorb more solar energy.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Describing the natural greenhouse effect
question_text: The natural greenhouse effect helps maintain Earth's habitability by _______?
answer: trapping some outgoing infrared radiation and warming the lower atmosphere

choices.text: trapping some outgoing infrared radiation and warming the lower atmosphere
blocking all solar radiation from reaching the surface
eliminating convection in the troposphere entirely
preventing water from evaporating from oceans

explanation: Greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, reducing net energy loss to space and keeping surface temperatures warmer than they would otherwise be.
common_mistake: The greenhouse effect involves infrared retention, not blocking all sunlight.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Identifying anthropogenic greenhouse gas sources
question_text: Which gas is a major anthropogenic contributor to enhanced greenhouse warming?
answer: carbon dioxide (CO₂) from fossil fuel combustion

choices.text: carbon dioxide (CO₂) from fossil fuel combustion
nitrogen (N₂) from the breakdown of inert rock only
argon (Ar) released by lightning exclusively
oxygen (O₂) produced by photosynthesis alone

explanation: CO₂ from burning coal, oil, and gas increases atmospheric concentration and enhances the greenhouse effect.
common_mistake: N₂ and Ar are not significant greenhouse gases; O₂ increase from photosynthesis is not the main warming driver.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Explaining methane's role in the greenhouse effect
question_text: Methane (CH₄) is considered a potent greenhouse gas because it?
answer: absorbs outgoing infrared radiation effectively despite lower atmospheric concentration than CO₂

choices.text: absorbs outgoing infrared radiation effectively despite lower atmospheric concentration than CO₂
reflects all incoming ultraviolet radiation back to space
replaces oxygen in the stratosphere entirely
has no interaction with electromagnetic radiation

explanation: Methane molecules absorb infrared wavelengths strongly; per molecule it has a higher warming potential than CO₂ over short time scales.
common_mistake: Greenhouse gases absorb infrared, not reflect UV; methane does interact with radiation.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Describing ozone layer protective function
question_text: The ozone layer in the stratosphere protects living organisms primarily by _______?
answer: absorbing harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the Sun

choices.text: absorbing harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the Sun
reflecting all visible light away from Earth
producing oxygen through nuclear fusion
preventing all infrared radiation from leaving Earth

explanation: Ozone (O₃) absorbs UV-B and UV-C, reducing DNA damage and other harmful effects at the surface.
common_mistake: Ozone targets UV radiation, not all visible light or outgoing infrared.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Explaining CFC impact on stratospheric ozone
question_text: Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) damaged the ozone layer because they _______?
answer: release chlorine atoms in the stratosphere that catalytically destroy ozone molecules

choices.text: release chlorine atoms in the stratosphere that catalytically destroy ozone molecules
directly reflect all solar radiation into space
increase Earth's albedo by forming permanent cloud cover only
convert ozone into visible light with no chemical change

explanation: UV radiation breaks down CFCs, freeing chlorine radicals that repeatedly destroy O₃ in catalytic cycles.
common_mistake: CFC harm is chemical destruction of ozone, not simple reflection.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Relating satellite monitoring to nature of scientific knowledge
question_text: Satellite monitoring of atmospheric gases illustrates that scientific understanding of climate is _______?
answer: built from collected evidence and revised as new data become available

choices.text: built from collected evidence and revised as new data become available
fixed permanently after a single experiment
based only on opinions without measurement
unrelated to technology or international cooperation

explanation: Modern climate science relies on long-term instrumental records and satellites, reflecting how science uses evidence over time.
common_mistake: Scientific knowledge in this area evolves with better data and models.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Distinguishing weather from climate
question_text: Weather differs from climate because weather describes?
answer: short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place

choices.text: short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place
average conditions over 30 years or more globally
only the type of biome found at a latitude
the total solar energy received by Earth in a year

explanation: Weather is immediate and local; climate is the long-term pattern of weather in a region.
common_mistake: Long-term averages define climate, not weather.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Defining climate using long-term data
question_text: A region's climate is best described by _______?
answer: long-term averages and patterns of temperature and precipitation

choices.text: long-term averages and patterns of temperature and precipitation
the temperature on a single winter day only
the wind direction during one thunderstorm
the daily cloud cover for one week only

explanation: Climate summaries use decades of data to characterize typical and extreme conditions.
common_mistake: Single-day or single-event data describe weather, not climate.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Explaining altitude as a climate factor
question_text: Cities at the same latitude but different elevations often have different climates because _______?
answer: temperature generally decreases with increasing altitude

choices.text: temperature generally decreases with increasing altitude
solar radiation cannot reach mountainous areas
precipitation never occurs above sea level
albedo is zero at all mountain summits

explanation: Higher elevation means thinner atmosphere and lower average temperatures, altering biome and climate type.
common_mistake: Mountains still receive solar radiation; elevation mainly changes temperature and precipitation patterns.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Explaining ocean current influence on regional climate
question_text: Ocean currents such as the Gulf Stream can affect coastal climate by _______?
answer: transporting warm or cold water that heats or cools adjacent air masses

choices.text: transporting warm or cold water that heats or cools adjacent air masses
blocking all solar radiation from reaching the coast
eliminating evaporation from the ocean surface
preventing any seasonal temperature change

explanation: Currents redistribute ocean heat, moderating or altering temperature and humidity of nearby land.
common_mistake: Currents transfer thermal energy; they do not stop solar input or evaporation entirely.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Identifying climatograph variables
question_text: A climatograph displays?
answer: average monthly temperature and precipitation for a location

choices.text: average monthly temperature and precipitation for a location
the daily wind speed for one week only
the chemical composition of soil horizons
the food web of a single pond ecosystem

explanation: Climatographs combine temperature (usually line graph) and precipitation (usually bars) by month.
common_mistake: Climatographs show climate variables over months, not soil chemistry or food webs.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Interpreting seasonal patterns on a climatograph
question_text: On a climatograph, a location with hot, wet summers and cold, dry winters would show?
answer: high precipitation bars and high temperature line in summer, low precipitation and low temperature in winter

choices.text: high precipitation bars and high temperature line in summer, low precipitation and low temperature in winter
identical temperature and precipitation every month
zero precipitation in all twelve months
temperature decreasing every month without variation

explanation: Seasonal climate patterns appear as repeating monthly differences in both temperature and precipitation.
common_mistake: Seasonal climates vary by month; flat lines and bars indicate little seasonal change.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Matching climatograph patterns to climate types
question_text: A climatograph with little monthly temperature variation and heavy rainfall every month is typical of _______?
answer: equatorial tropical rainforest climate

choices.text: equatorial tropical rainforest climate
polar desert climate
mid-latitude steppe climate
high-latitude tundra climate

explanation: Tropical rainforests near the equator stay warm year-round with consistently high precipitation.
common_mistake: Polar and tundra climates have low temperatures; steppe climates are drier.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Defining biome classification basis
question_text: Biomes are large regions classified mainly by _______?
answer: climate and characteristic vegetation (and associated animal life)

choices.text: climate and characteristic vegetation (and associated animal life)
the age of bedrock only
the political borders of countries
the phase of the Moon during winter

explanation: Climate (temperature and precipitation) largely determines which biome develops in a region.
common_mistake: Biomes are ecological, not political or geological age categories.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Relating latitude to biome distribution
question_text: As latitude increases from the equator toward the poles, terrestrial biomes generally shift from _______?
answer: tropical forest toward temperate forest, grassland, and tundra

choices.text: tropical forest toward temperate forest, grassland, and tundra
desert toward tropical rainforest only
tundra toward equatorial savanna without change in temperature
marine biome toward desert with no precipitation change

explanation: Lower average solar angle and cooler temperatures at high latitudes support different biome types than the equator.
common_mistake: Biome zones change with latitude due to changing climate, not randomly.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Identifying boreal forest characteristics
question_text: The boreal forest (taiga) biome is characterized by _______?
answer: long, cold winters and coniferous trees such as spruce and pine

choices.text: long, cold winters and coniferous trees such as spruce and pine
year-round temperatures above 25 °C and cacti
no trees and permanently frozen soil only with no summer thaw
coral reefs and mangrove swamps

explanation: Taiga occurs in subarctic regions with short summers and needle-leaf conifers adapted to cold.
common_mistake: Hot deserts, pure ice sheets, and coral reefs belong to other biomes.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Describing grassland climate and vegetation
question_text: Grassland biomes typically experience?
answer: moderate rainfall insufficient to support continuous forest cover

choices.text: moderate rainfall insufficient to support continuous forest cover
constant heavy rainfall supporting tropical rainforest trees
no precipitation and no growing season at any time
permanently frozen ground with no grasses

explanation: Grasslands occupy a precipitation range between desert and forest, supporting grasses and few trees.
common_mistake: Grasslands receive enough rain for grasses but not dense forests.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Identifying desert biome defining features
question_text: Desert biomes are defined primarily by _______?
answer: low annual precipitation and adaptations for water conservation

choices.text: low annual precipitation and adaptations for water conservation
continuous snow cover and permafrost
constant daily rainfall throughout the year
submerged saline conditions with kelp forests

explanation: Deserts receive very little precipitation; plants and animals show drought adaptations.
common_mistake: Snow, constant rain, and kelp forests describe other biomes.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Describing open-ocean marine biome features
question_text: The open-ocean zone of a marine biome is characterized by _______?
answer: deep water far from shore with plankton and nekton adapted to open water

choices.text: deep water far from shore with plankton and nekton adapted to open water
daily exposure to air at low tide only
freshwater with no dissolved salts
permanently frozen soil and low shrubs

explanation: Pelagic (open-ocean) life includes drifting plankton and swimming organisms in saline water.
common_mistake: Intertidal, freshwater, and tundra describe different environments.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Explaining ecological importance of estuaries
question_text: Estuaries where freshwater rivers meet the ocean are important aquatic biomes because they _______?
answer: provide nutrient-rich nursery habitat with mixed salinity conditions

choices.text: provide nutrient-rich nursery habitat with mixed salinity conditions
contain no life due to saltwater and freshwater mixing
remain frozen year-round at all latitudes
lack any connection to solar energy or food webs

explanation: Estuaries support high productivity and serve as breeding grounds for many species.
common_mistake: Estuaries are biologically rich, not lifeless or permanently frozen.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Interpreting ice cores as climate change evidence
question_text: Ice core data provide evidence of past climate change because trapped bubbles contain?
answer: samples of ancient atmospheric gases such as CO₂ and methane

choices.text: samples of ancient atmospheric gases such as CO₂ and methane
only modern air from the drilling process
fossilized plants from the current century only
pure oxygen with no other gases ever present

explanation: Layered ice preserves air from when each layer formed, allowing comparison of greenhouse gas levels over millennia.
common_mistake: Ice cores contain trapped ancient air, not only contamination from drilling.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Identifying evidence and mechanisms of sea level rise
question_text: Global mean sea level has risen in recent decades partly because _______?
answer: thermal expansion of warming ocean water and melting land ice add water to oceans

choices.text: thermal expansion of warming ocean water and melting land ice add water to oceans
Earth's total amount of water is increasing from nuclear reactions in the sea
all rivers have stopped flowing into the oceans
evaporation has permanently removed all ocean water

explanation: Warming increases water volume and melts glaciers and ice sheets, raising sea level.
common_mistake: Sea level rise involves existing water expanding and ice melting, not creating new water.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Distinguishing natural volcanic cooling from long-term warming
question_text: Volcanic eruptions can cause short-term cooling by _______?
answer: injecting aerosols and ash that reflect incoming solar radiation

choices.text: injecting aerosols and ash that reflect incoming solar radiation
adding large amounts of stratospheric ozone instantly
eliminating all greenhouse gases permanently
increasing Earth's albedo to exactly 1.0 forever

explanation: Volcanic aerosols reflect sunlight temporarily; effects usually last months to a few years, unlike long-term CO₂ warming.
common_mistake: Volcanic cooling is temporary reflection, not permanent removal of all greenhouse gases.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Linking fossil fuel use to anthropogenic warming
question_text: Burning fossil fuels contributes to climate change mainly by _______?
answer: increasing atmospheric CO₂ concentration and enhancing the greenhouse effect

choices.text: increasing atmospheric CO₂ concentration and enhancing the greenhouse effect
decreasing atmospheric nitrogen to zero
reflecting all incoming solar radiation with ash only
creating new water molecules that cannot absorb heat

explanation: Fossil fuel combustion releases stored carbon as CO₂, a long-lived greenhouse gas.
common_mistake: The main issue is CO₂ accumulation, not nitrogen loss or total solar reflection.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Describing regional climate change impacts
question_text: One predicted effect of continued global warming in Alberta includes _______?
answer: more frequent extreme weather events and shifting ecosystems

choices.text: more frequent extreme weather events and shifting ecosystems
complete elimination of all winter seasons immediately
permanent freezing of all prairie farmland year-round
removal of all greenhouse gases from the atmosphere naturally within days

explanation: Climate models project hotter extremes, altered precipitation, and biome shifts; changes occur over years to decades.
common_mistake: Extremes increase and patterns shift; instant permanent ice or instant gas removal is unrealistic.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Explaining ocean acidification consequences
question_text: Ocean acidification linked to increased CO₂ affects marine ecosystems by _______?
answer: lowering pH and reducing availability of carbonate ions for shell-building organisms

choices.text: lowering pH and reducing availability of carbonate ions for shell-building organisms
increasing pH and strengthening all coral reefs without limit
eliminating all dissolved gases from seawater
preventing any photosynthesis by phytoplankton globally

explanation: CO₂ dissolves to form carbonic acid, harming calcifying organisms such as corals and some plankton.
common_mistake: Acidification lowers pH; it does not strengthen shells or stop all photosynthesis.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Defining climate change mitigation strategies
question_text: Mitigation of climate change refers to actions that?
answer: reduce greenhouse gas emissions or enhance carbon sinks

choices.text: reduce greenhouse gas emissions or enhance carbon sinks
only adapt infrastructure after damage has occurred
ignore atmospheric gases and focus solely on weather forecasts
increase fossil fuel use to reflect sunlight

explanation: Mitigation addresses causes (e.g., renewable energy, reforestation); adaptation handles impacts.
common_mistake: Adaptation is different from mitigation; mitigation reduces emissions or captures carbon.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Distinguishing adaptation from mitigation
question_text: Planting drought-resistant crop varieties in Alberta is an example of _______?
answer: adaptation to expected climate change impacts

choices.text: adaptation to expected climate change impacts
mitigation that removes CO₂ from the atmosphere directly
evidence that climate never changes naturally
elimination of the greenhouse effect entirely

explanation: Adaptation adjusts practices to cope with new conditions without necessarily lowering emissions.
common_mistake: Crop choice adapts to change; it is not the same as emission reduction (mitigation).

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Analyzing societal response to climate change
question_text: International climate agreements reflect the STS idea that addressing global warming requires _______?
answer: cooperation among governments, scientists, and citizens using shared data

choices.text: cooperation among governments, scientists, and citizens using shared data
decisions by one person without any evidence
ignoring developing nations' energy needs entirely
avoiding all measurement of atmospheric gases

explanation: Climate change is global; policy responses involve international negotiation and scientific input.
common_mistake: Effective response needs collaboration and evidence, not isolation or ignoring data.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Distinguishing heat from temperature
question_text: Temperature and heat differ because temperature measures _______?
answer: average kinetic energy of particles, while heat is energy transferred due to a temperature difference

choices.text: average kinetic energy of particles, while heat is energy transferred due to a temperature difference
total mass of an object only
the colour of radiation emitted by a surface only
the amount of oxygen in the atmosphere

explanation: Temperature is a property indicating particle motion; heat (thermal energy transfer) flows from higher to lower temperature.
common_mistake: Heat is energy in transit; temperature is not the same as total thermal energy.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Relating thermal energy to mass and material
question_text: Two objects at the same temperature may contain different amounts of thermal energy if they have _______?
answer: different masses or different specific heat capacities

choices.text: different masses or different specific heat capacities
identical chemical formulas always
the same colour and shape only
zero kinetic energy in all particles

explanation: Thermal energy depends on mass, specific heat, and temperature: Q = mcΔT for a given change.
common_mistake: Same temperature does not mean same total thermal energy for different masses or materials.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Explaining significance of water's specific heat capacity
question_text: Water has a high specific heat capacity, which means it?
answer: absorbs or releases large amounts of energy with relatively small temperature changes

choices.text: absorbs or releases large amounts of energy with relatively small temperature changes
cannot store any thermal energy in oceans
always boils at 0 °C at standard pressure
transfers heat only by conduction, never by radiation

explanation: High c means more energy is needed per kilogram to change temperature, stabilizing coastal and global climates.
common_mistake: High specific heat increases thermal storage and moderates temperature swings.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Comparing heating rates using specific heat capacity
question_text: Sand heats up faster than water on a sunny day primarily because sand?
answer: has a lower specific heat capacity than water

choices.text: has a lower specific heat capacity than water
absorbs no solar radiation at all
has a higher specific heat capacity than water
cannot transfer energy by any mechanism

explanation: Lower c means temperature rises more quickly for the same energy input per kilogram.
common_mistake: Sand heats faster due to lower specific heat, not because it absorbs no radiation.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Identifying heat transfer mechanisms in the atmosphere
question_text: Energy transfer in the atmosphere that drives wind and storm systems includes _______?
answer: radiation, conduction, and convection of thermal energy

choices.text: radiation, conduction, and convection of thermal energy
only chemical bonds breaking in solid rock with no air movement
transfer of matter without any energy change
gravitational energy from the Moon stopping all air motion

explanation: Solar heating creates temperature differences; convection moves warm air and moisture, producing weather.
common_mistake: Weather involves energy transfer in fluids (convection) and radiation from Sun and Earth.

unit: Energy Flow in Global Systems
topic: Energy Flow in Global Systems
skill_tested: Explaining atmospheric convection from surface heating
question_text: When sunlight warms the ground, the air above it often rises because _______?
answer: heated air expands, becomes less dense, and convection carries energy upward

choices.text: heated air expands, becomes less dense, and convection carries energy upward
cold air is always less dense than warm air
conduction cannot occur in gases under any condition
radiation stops once the surface is warmed

explanation: Convection transfers energy vertically in fluids when warm, low-density regions rise.
common_mistake: Warm air is less dense; convection complements conduction and radiation.

unit: Energy Flow in Technological Systems
